Title: The Orthotospovirus nonstructural protein NSs suppresses plant MYC-regulated jasmonate signaling leading to enhanced vector attraction and performance

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Orthotospovirus nonstructural protein; viral silencing suppressor/effector
Common_Genes.Source_Species: Tomato spotted wilt orthotospovirus; Tomato zonate spot orthotospovirus
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Capsicum annuum; Nicotiana benthamiana
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Repel thrips and contribute to anti-vector plant defense
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NSs suppresses jasmonate-regulated terpene biosynthesis by binding MYC TFs, thereby reducing repellent monoterpenes and increasing thrips attraction and performance
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: TSWV or TZSV infection; MeJA treatment; thrips infestation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: heterologous expression with PVX; Arabidopsis 35S:YFP-NSs transgenics; yeast two-hybrid; BiFC; GST pull-down; co-immunoprecipitation; RT-qPCR; volatile GC-MS; thrips choice and spawning assays
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NSs alone is sufficient to increase pepper and Arabidopsis attractiveness to thrips. 2. NSs directly interacts with MYC2/3/4 and suppresses JA-mediated activation of terpene synthase genes. 3. NSs expression lowers defensive monoterpene output and enhances thrips offspring production. 4. MYC2 targeting is conserved for TSWV and TZSV NSs.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A key anti-virulence target; blocking NSs accumulation or its interaction with MYC factors could preserve volatile defenses and reduce vector-mediated virus spread
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Ectopic expression sufficient to alter defense and vector behavior
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although not a plant gene, NSs is the central experimentally validated causal factor connecting pathogen infection to altered nutrient/secondary metabolite output and vector behavior.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts geranyl diphosphate into the monoterpene volatile β-ocimene
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Repels western flower thrips and contributes to volatile-based plant defense
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of AtTPS10 increases Arabidopsis attractiveness and suitability to thrips, indicating AtTPS10-promoted β-ocimene contributes to anti-thrips defense
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Promoter activity strongly induced at thrips feeding sites after 24 h infestation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: tps10-1 mutant; promoter:GUS assay; thrips two-choice assay; pure β-ocimene behavioral assay; thrips spawning assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtTPS10 promoter activity is induced by thrips feeding. 2. tps10-1 mutants are more attractive to thrips than wild type. 3. β-ocimene repels thrips, supporting AtTPS10-mediated volatile defense. 4. tps10-1 plants support increased thrips offspring.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Enhancing TPS10 activity or deploying β-ocimene-emitting lines may improve volatile-based resistance to thrips and potentially reduce virus transmission
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: AtTPS10 promoter:GUS (native promoter reporter); 35S used elsewhere in study but not for AtTPS10
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper explicitly states AtTPS10 encodes a monoterpene synthase producing β-ocimene; detailed enzyme biochemistry was not newly characterized here.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Participates in monoterpene synthesis in pepper leaves
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression is induced by thrips but suppressed in TSWV-infected plants, consistent with reduced defensive monoterpene emission and higher thrips attraction
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated after thrips infestation; induction suppressed by TSWV infection
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; volatile profiling; thrips preference assay at whole-plant level
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CaMTS1 is induced by thrips infestation. 2. TSWV infection suppresses its induction. 3. Suppression is associated with reduced monoterpene-based defense and greater thrips attraction.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate marker/gene for enhancing pepper volatile defense against thrips and vector-borne virus spread
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific catalytic product was not assigned in this paper; included because these TPS genes are primary experimental subjects linking virus to monoterpene defense.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate target for breeding pepper lines with stronger terpene-mediated vector repellence
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific product not assigned in this paper.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Participates in terpene biosynthesis induced by jasmonate and thrips feeding
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression is induced by thrips and MeJA, but this induction is reduced in TSWV-infected plants, consistent with lower monoterpene emission
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced by thrips infestation and MeJA; suppressed by TSWV under MeJA treatment
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: thrips infestation; MeJA treatment; TSWV infection
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NbTPS5 responds to thrips and MeJA. 2. TSWV dampens this induction. 3. Reduced expression correlates with lower defensive monoterpene emission.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate marker for strengthening jasmonate-inducible volatile defenses in solanaceous crops
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No direct mutant or enzyme assay for NbTPS5 in this paper.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Participates in terpene biosynthesis induced by jasmonate and thrips feeding
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression is induced by thrips and MeJA, but this induction is reduced in TSWV-infected plants, consistent with lower monoterpene emission
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced by thrips infestation and MeJA; suppressed by TSWV under MeJA treatment
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: thrips infestation; MeJA treatment; TSWV infection
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NbTPS38 responds to thrips and MeJA. 2. TSWV dampens this induction. 3. Reduced expression correlates with lower defensive monoterpene emission.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential engineering target for increasing anti-thrips volatile output
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No direct mutant or enzyme assay for NbTPS38 in this paper.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Repel thrips and mediate volatile-based defense
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates terpene synthase genes including AtTPS10
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: JA-signaling master regulator essential for terpene-dependent immunity against thrips and direct viral target
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: MYC2 promotes terpene-based defense; loss of MYC2 or NSs-mediated inhibition increases thrips attraction and performance
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Promoter activity induced at thrips feeding sites after 24 h infestation
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions within JA transcriptional regulatory network; no specific complex experimentally resolved here
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: thrips infestation; MeJA treatment; TSWV/TZSV infection context
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: yeast two-hybrid; BiFC; GST pull-down; co-immunoprecipitation; myc2-1 mutant; promoter:GUS assay; thrips preference and spawning assays
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtMYC2 directly interacts with TSWV NSs in vitro and in vivo. 2. AtMYC2 promoter activity is induced by thrips feeding. 3. myc2-1 mutants are more attractive to thrips and support more offspring. 4. NSs suppresses MYC2-mediated terpene defense, enhancing vector attraction and performance. 5. TZSV NSs also interacts with AtMYC2, indicating conservation.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Protecting MYC2 function or preventing NSs-MYC2 interaction could sustain jasmonate-dependent volatile defense and reduce vector-borne virus transmission
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper identifies MYC2 as a convergent host target of multiple plant virus effectors.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Acts redundantly with MYC2/MYC4 to activate JA responses including terpene defense outputs
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Contributes redundantly to the MYC defense module targeted by NSs; loss in myc234 strongly increases thrips preference
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Targeted by NSs and contributes to terpene-dependent anti-thrips immunity
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytoplasm and nucleus when interacting with NSs in BiFC
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: thrips infestation; virus effector interaction context
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: yeast two-hybrid; BiFC; myc234 triple mutant preference/spawning assays
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtMYC3 interacts with TSWV NSs. 2. Together with MYC2 and MYC4 it redundantly supports anti-thrips defense. 3. Loss of the MYC module increases thrips attraction and performance.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential co-target with MYC2/MYC4 for preserving JA-dependent defense robustness against viral suppression
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Studied mainly as part of the redundant MYC module rather than individually for downstream target specificity.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Acts redundantly with MYC2/MYC3 to activate JA responses including terpene defense outputs
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Contributes redundantly to the MYC defense module targeted by NSs; loss in myc234 strongly increases thrips preference
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Targeted by NSs and contributes to terpene-dependent anti-thrips immunity
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytoplasm when interacting with NSs in BiFC
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: thrips infestation; virus effector interaction context
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: yeast two-hybrid; BiFC; myc234 triple mutant preference/spawning assays
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtMYC4 interacts with TSWV NSs. 2. Together with MYC2 and MYC3 it redundantly supports anti-thrips defense. 3. Loss of the MYC module increases thrips attraction and performance.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential co-target with MYC2/MYC3 for improving resilience of JA-regulated volatile defense
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Studied mainly as part of the redundant MYC module rather than individually for downstream target specificity.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Presumed activation of pepper TPS genes based on orthology and interaction evidence
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Pepper MYC2 ortholog is a conserved viral target likely linking NSs to reduced terpene synthase expression and increased vector attraction in pepper
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Interacts with TSWV NSs and TZSV NSs, implying suppression of pepper JA-dependent monoterpene defense
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Pepper CaMYC2 physically interacts with TSWV NSs in vivo. 2. CaMYC2 also interacts with TZSV NSs. 3. The NSs-MYC2 interaction is therefore conserved across host species and orthotospoviruses.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for breeding or engineering pepper resistance by disrupting NSs-CaMYC2 interaction while preserving JA defense outputs
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct genetic validation of CaMYC2 function in pepper metabolism was not performed in this paper, but its relevance is strongly supported by conserved interaction and pepper phenotype data.
